title: Swiss roll with jam and cream
subtitle: Classic rolled sponge with strawberry jam and whipped cream
excerpt: A thin sponge baked hot and fast, spread with jam and whipped cream, and rolled while still warm. The warmth is what keeps it from cracking. A ten-minute bake, but the timing has to be right.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — Swiss roll documented as a thin, rolled sponge spread with jam. The technique of rolling the sponge immediately after baking, while still warm, to prevent cracking is consistent across British domestic cookery from the nineteenth century onwards.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day it is made. The cream softens the sponge after a few hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, top third — hot and fast to set the sponge before it dries out

glossaryTerms.definition: Turning a Swiss roll sponge onto sugared paper and rolling it up immediately when it comes from the oven, before the sponge has set. Warm sponge is pliable; cool sponge is brittle and will crack. The roll is done with the paper as a temporary filling, which holds the curl until the sponge is cool enough to be filled with jam and cream.

content.text: The only real skill in a Swiss roll is speed.

is what prevents cracking: as soon as the tin comes out of the oven, the sponge must be turned out onto sugared paper and rolled up with the paper inside. Have the sugared paper ready before the sponge goes in.
Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Grease a 23×33 cm Swiss roll tin and line with baking paper. Lay a second sheet of baking paper on the worktop and dust it generously with {{caster-sugar}}.

Beat the {{free-range-eggs}}, 75 g of {{caster-sugar}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} with a hand mixer at high speed for 4–5 minutes until the mixture is very pale, thick, and triples in volume. The batter should hold a ribbon for 3 seconds when the beaters are lifted. Fold in the sifted {{plain-flour}} in two additions with a large metal spoon, cutting through rather than stirring, until no flour streaks remain. Pour into the tin and spread to the corners.

Bake for 10–12 minutes until the sponge is pale gold and springs back when pressed. Work quickly: immediately invert the tin onto the sugared paper and peel off the lining paper. Roll the sponge up from the short end with the sugared paper inside. Leave to cool completely — about 30 minutes.

Carefully unroll the sponge. Spread the {{strawberry-jam}} over the surface to within 2 cm of the far edge. Spread the whipped {{double-cream}} over the jam. Re-roll the sponge from the same end, without the paper. Dust with extra caster sugar before serving.
